vulns.title: PHP-CGI argument injection with public watchTowr exploit
vulns.summary: PHP-CGI argument injection on affected Windows locale deployments gives a practical remote code execution path with a public researcher poc.
vulns.breakdown: The useful signal is not the CVSS number; it is the narrow but very real deployment pattern. Hunt for PHP-CGI behind Apache/XAMPP-style stacks, especially externally reachable Windows hosts using affected locales or equivalent argument parsing exposure.
vulns.whatBroke: PHP-CGI argument handling could be coerced through Windows character conversion behavior, turning request parameters into interpreter switches.
vulns.reality: Realistic against the right PHP-CGI deployment shape. The public watchTowr poc is a useful validator, but broad scanning still needs environment checks.
vulns.chains: Initial web RCE, webshell placement, credential collection, lateral movement from legacy Windows web hosts, and follow-on identity abuse.
vulns.weaponized: Likely and already attractive because the bug sits on internet-facing web stacks and has a clean public exploit path.

vulns.title: AD CS template abuse remains chainable identity infrastructure debt
vulns.summary: Misconfigured certificate templates and enrollment paths continue to turn ordinary domain access into durable identity escalation opportunities.
vulns.breakdown: Track template enrollment rights, manager approval bypasses, ESC-style paths, NTLM relay to certificate endpoints, and whether recent hardening changed exploit reliability in the target estate.
vulns.whatBroke: Certificate trust and enrollment policy can permit authentication material issuance beyond intended privilege boundaries.
vulns.reality: Highly realistic where templates, web enrollment, or relay conditions line up. This is configuration-driven, not patch-only.
vulns.chains: NTLM relay, coercion, Kerberos abuse, low-priv domain footholds, and persistence through certificate lifetime.
vulns.weaponized: Already operationalized in mature tooling. The variable is target configuration, not exploit state.

vulns.title: Linux nf_tables UAF LPE with public repro chain
vulns.summary: nf_tables use-after-free research remains useful for local privilege escalation triage on affected Linux kernels with public exploit code available.
vulns.breakdown: The operational value is kernel exposure mapping, namespace/container preconditions, and whether the target fleet carries vulnerable kernel lines. Treat pocs as environment-sensitive but worth validating in lab images that mirror production.
vulns.whatBroke: A kernel memory lifetime bug in nf_tables can permit local code to shape a privilege escalation primitive.
vulns.reality: Realistic where affected kernels and required local execution conditions line up. The exploit path is public, but reliability depends on kernel build and mitigations.
vulns.chains: Initial foothold, container escape research, local privilege escalation, and persistence setup after low-priv execution.
vulns.weaponized: Likely in targeted post-exploitation workflows. Less useful as a drive-by issue, valuable once an operator already has code execution.

vulns.title: Android run-as package installer forgery with public bypass poc
vulns.summary: Android PackageInstaller input validation can be abused from an adb foothold to run code as another installed app, which makes it useful for app-data access and device-lab privilege testing.
vulns.breakdown: This is not remote initial access, but it is useful mobile tradecraft once an operator has adb, local debugging access, or a chained device foothold. The real value is crossing app UID boundaries and validating whether fleet devices carry the vulnerable framework behavior.
vulns.whatBroke: PackageInstaller session creation accepted crafted input that crossed into the downstream run-as path and confused which app identity should be used.
vulns.reality: Realistic under local/adb preconditions. The public bypass poc is repeatable for validation on affected Android builds.
vulns.chains: ADB access, malicious local app workflow, app data access, token theft from targeted apps, and follow-on mobile persistence testing.
vulns.weaponized: Likely as a post-access mobile primitive. It is not broad remote exploitation, but it is practical in device labs and chained workflows.

vulns.title: iOS kfd kernel r/w primitive with public exploit library
vulns.summary: Public kfd-style exploit code provides kernel memory access primitives on supported iOS versions, with value depending heavily on device model, build, and exploit method.
vulns.breakdown: This belongs in mobile triage because iOS kernel primitives are chain material, not because they are universally push-button. Track supported device/version ranges, installation preconditions, and whether a public primitive can pair with CoreTrust, WebKit, or sandbox escape work.
vulns.whatBroke: An Apple kernel integer overflow path could be shaped into kernel privilege code execution or kernel memory read/write under the right conditions.
vulns.reality: Realistic in supported lab/device conditions. Reliability is version-specific and should be validated against exact hardware and iOS builds.
vulns.chains: WebKit or app foothold, sandbox escape, kernel r/w primitive, CoreTrust-style signing bypass, jailbreak tooling, and mobile implant research.
vulns.weaponized: Likely in high-end chains, but public tooling is mostly useful for research, validation, and jailbreak-adjacent workflows.

vulns.title: TeamCity auth bypass to CI server code execution
vulns.summary: JetBrains TeamCity authentication bypass remains operator-relevant because CI servers often sit near secrets, deployment keys, and build trust.
vulns.breakdown: The exploit path is valuable because the target is not just an app server; it is a software supply-chain control point. Prioritize exposed TeamCity, forgotten agents, and credential material available to build runners.
vulns.whatBroke: A request path allowed authentication bypass against TeamCity administrative surfaces.
vulns.reality: Realistic where exposed instances lag patching. Public exploit code and scanner checks make validation straightforward.
vulns.chains: CI secrets theft, build pipeline compromise, package tampering, cloud credential reuse, and lateral movement through deployment automation.
vulns.weaponized: Likely. CI products convert initial access into broad environment leverage quickly.

vulns.title: ScreenConnect authentication bypass with instant fleet value
vulns.summary: ScreenConnect auth bypass is high-signal because RMM compromise often means privileged access across many downstream machines.
vulns.breakdown: Operator value is in blast radius: remote management tools collapse many endpoints into one control plane. Prioritize internet exposure, unknown admin creation, and historical persistence left by opportunistic exploitation.
vulns.whatBroke: A setup/authentication flow could be abused to gain administrative control without valid credentials.
vulns.reality: Very realistic on exposed and unpatched appliances. public pocs made opportunistic validation trivial.
vulns.chains: RMM control, endpoint command execution, credential theft, lateral movement, and MSP-style downstream compromise.
vulns.weaponized: Already weaponized in broad scanning and intrusion workflows.

vulns.title: Jenkins CLI file read that chains into controller compromise
vulns.summary: Jenkins arbitrary file read is useful when it exposes secrets, crumb issuers, credentials, or plugin state that can be chained into execution.
vulns.breakdown: Treat this as a chain primitive rather than a one-shot RCE. The interesting question is what sensitive files are readable in the target controller context and whether those secrets unlock job execution or admin paths.
vulns.whatBroke: Jenkins CLI argument expansion allowed unauthorized file content disclosure from the controller.
vulns.reality: Realistic as an information disclosure and chain step. Exploit quality depends on target hardening and reachable secrets.
vulns.chains: Credential recovery, Jenkins admin takeover, build job execution, SCM token theft, and cloud deployment key abuse.
vulns.weaponized: Likely as a chaining primitive. It is especially valuable on CI systems with broad secrets.

vulns.title: Citrix Bleed session theft with durable edge-access value
vulns.summary: Citrix ADC/Gateway session disclosure stays relevant because stolen sessions can bypass normal credential and MFA assumptions.
vulns.breakdown: This is an access primitive. Look for historic compromise, active sessions created before patching, persistence after appliance remediation, and whether session theft fed downstream identity abuse.
vulns.whatBroke: A memory disclosure issue exposed sensitive session material from Citrix ADC/Gateway.
vulns.reality: Realistic against affected edge appliances. public pocs made validation and theft workflows accessible.
vulns.chains: Session hijack, VPN access, internal discovery, credential collection, and identity pivoting.
vulns.weaponized: Already observed and operationally valuable because it bypasses password-centric thinking.

vulns.title: OpenSSH regreSSHion as high-friction but important RCE research
vulns.summary: OpenSSH signal-handler race research matters because SSH exposure is universal, even when exploitation is timing-sensitive and target-dependent.
vulns.breakdown: This belongs in the archive because it is not easy-button exploitation, but the blast radius and service ubiquity are too important to ignore. Track exploit improvements, target architecture reliability, and distro-specific exposure.
vulns.whatBroke: A regression reintroduced an unsafe signal-handler race condition in sshd.
vulns.reality: Realistic in lab and select environments; difficult at scale without target-specific reliability work.
vulns.chains: Internet SSH exposure, unauthenticated foothold attempts, post-exploit privilege context, and infrastructure-wide scanning.
vulns.weaponized: Possible when reliability improves. Treat current public pocs as validation signals, not guaranteed commodity exploitation.

vulns.title: FortiOS SSL-VPN RCE on exposed perimeter appliances
vulns.summary: FortiOS SSL-VPN bugs stay high priority because exposed VPN appliances create direct edge-to-internal pivot opportunities.
vulns.breakdown: Focus on version exposure, SSL-VPN enablement, appliance logs, and whether public exploit variants are converging into reliable checks versus noisy crashers.
vulns.whatBroke: A FortiOS SSL-VPN vulnerability could permit code execution under specific appliance conditions.
vulns.reality: Realistic for vulnerable exposed appliances, but poc reliability varies by build and target condition.
vulns.chains: Perimeter RCE, credential harvesting, VPN trust abuse, internal discovery, and persistence on network edge.
vulns.weaponized: Likely when reliable exploit variants are available. Edge appliance bugs are routinely folded into operator workflows.

vulns.title: Citrix ADC unauthenticated RCE with proven intrusion history
vulns.summary: Unauthenticated Citrix ADC/Gateway RCE remains archetypal edge-device risk: exploitable perimeter code with immediate internal-access implications.
vulns.breakdown: Archive value is in exploit archaeology and historical compromise review. Even patched estates need incident review for webshells, appliance persistence, and stolen credentials.
vulns.whatBroke: Citrix ADC/Gateway exposed an unauthenticated path to code execution on vulnerable appliances.
vulns.reality: Highly realistic on affected exposed systems. The vulnerability has public exploit paths and strong historical exploitation signal.
vulns.chains: Perimeter foothold, webshell, credential theft, internal pivoting, and appliance persistence.
vulns.weaponized: Already weaponized; the main question is lingering compromise, not exploit possibility.

vulns.title: GoAnywhere auth bypass with file-transfer blast radius
vulns.summary: Managed file transfer bugs deserve archive space because they often sit near sensitive data flows and partner trust boundaries.
vulns.breakdown: Treat this as data-access and control-plane risk. Public exploitability matters less than what the application stores, transfers, and authenticates to downstream.
vulns.whatBroke: An authentication bypass condition exposed administrative or sensitive application paths.
vulns.reality: Realistic against vulnerable deployments. public pocs make validation straightforward.
vulns.chains: Data theft, credential recovery, partner trust abuse, scheduled transfer tampering, and follow-on webshell or admin actions.
vulns.weaponized: Likely in data theft operations due to the target class.

vulns.title: Confluence data-center takeover path with public exploit flow
vulns.summary: Confluence remains a high-signal target when public exploit flows lead to administrative control or content-backed credential exposure.
vulns.breakdown: Prioritize exposed Confluence, backup/restore behavior, app links, stored credentials, and marketplace plugin blast radius.
vulns.whatBroke: A vulnerable Confluence path enabled improper control of instance state and administrative outcomes.
vulns.reality: Realistic on exposed vulnerable instances. Public exploit automation exists and is easy to validate.
vulns.chains: Admin takeover, user directory access, stored secrets, plugin deployment, and internal documentation mining.
vulns.weaponized: Likely and historically common due to Confluence's enterprise footprint.

vulns.title: MOVEit Transfer SQLi-to-RCE exploitation archeology
vulns.summary: MOVEit remains a useful archive anchor for file-transfer exploitation, data theft workflows, and web app compromise at enterprise scale.
vulns.breakdown: Track this as exploit archaeology: the target class, webshell deployment pattern, and data-theft motivation matter more than just the CVE mechanics.
vulns.whatBroke: SQL injection in MOVEit Transfer enabled unauthorized access and paths toward code execution or data extraction.
vulns.reality: Realistic against vulnerable exposed systems. Public exploit workflows are mature.
vulns.chains: SQLi, webshell, file inventory, bulk exfiltration, credential reuse, and extortion operations.
vulns.weaponized: Already weaponized and historically exploited at scale.

vulns.title: GeoServer property expression RCE with public exploit pressure
vulns.summary: GeoServer RCE deserves archive attention because internet-facing geospatial stacks are often neglected but hold privileged data access.
vulns.breakdown: The operational question is exposure and data sensitivity. Public exploit pressure means even niche middleware belongs in daily triage when exposed.
vulns.whatBroke: Unsafe property expression handling exposed remote code execution in vulnerable GeoServer deployments.
vulns.reality: Realistic on exposed vulnerable servers. pocs are public and validation is straightforward.
vulns.chains: Web RCE, data store credential theft, internal database reach, cloud credential discovery, and webshell persistence.
vulns.weaponized: Likely where exposed. Niche software becomes high-value when internet-facing and scriptable.

vulns.title: Adobe ColdFusion arbitrary file read as credential-recovery chain step
vulns.summary: ColdFusion file read issues are useful when they expose admin secrets, datasource credentials, or configuration that unlocks execution paths.
vulns.breakdown: Treat as a chain primitive. The value comes from what the server stores: datasource passwords, admin hashes, templates, and integration secrets.
vulns.whatBroke: A vulnerable ColdFusion path allowed unauthorized file disclosure under certain conditions.
vulns.reality: Realistic as a file disclosure primitive. Execution depends on discovered secrets and reachable admin workflows.
vulns.chains: File read, credential recovery, admin login, scheduled task abuse, template upload, and database pivot.
vulns.weaponized: Moderately likely because ColdFusion has a long history of reliable exploitation chains.
